exercises.example_sentence: クラスの中で田中さんが一番背が高いです。
exercises.translation: Tanaka is the tallest in the class.
wrong_choice_explanations: のほうが marks one side of a two-way comparison ('Tanaka is taller'), but this sentence ranks Tanaka at the top of a whole group (the class), which calls for 一番.
よりも means 'more than [something],' and it needs a second item as the standard of comparison — here we are picking Tanaka out of the whole class, not comparing him to one named person.
だけが would mean 'only Tanaka is tall in the class,' but the sentence is saying he is the tallest among many, not the only tall one.
exercises.hiragana_full: くらすのなかでたなかさんがいちばんせがたかいです。

audio_alternatives.kanji: うちのクラスで一番背が高い人は田中さんです。
audio_alternatives.hiragana_full: うちのくらすでいちばんせがたかいひとはたなかさんです。

audio_alternatives.kanji: 田中さんはクラスのみんなより背が高い。
audio_alternatives.hiragana_full: たなかさんはくらすのみんなよりせがたかい。

exercises.example_sentence: 果物の中でりんごが一番安いです。
exercises.translation: Out of all the fruits, apples are the cheapest.
wrong_choice_explanations: のほうが would set up a two-way comparison ('apples are cheaper'), but the sentence is ranking apples at the bottom of the price scale among all fruits, which requires 一番.
よりも means 'more than [something]' and needs an explicit second item; here we are picking the cheapest out of the whole fruit category, not comparing to one named fruit.
だけが would mean 'only apples are cheap among the fruits,' but the sentence is saying apples are the cheapest of many fruits — other fruits may also be cheap, just not as cheap.
exercises.hiragana_full: くだもののなかでりんごがいちばんやすいです。

audio_alternatives.kanji: 果物の中で一番安いのはりんごです。
audio_alternatives.hiragana_full: くだもののなかでいちばんやすいのはりんごです。

audio_alternatives.kanji: 他の果物より、りんごのほうがずっと安い。
audio_alternatives.hiragana_full: ほかのくだものより、りんごのほうがずっとやすい。

exercises.example_sentence: 家族の中で母が一番早く起きます。
exercises.translation: Out of my family, my mom wakes up the earliest.
wrong_choice_explanations: のほうが would set up a two-way comparison ('mom wakes up earlier'), but the sentence is picking mom out of the whole family, which requires 一番.
よりも means 'earlier than [someone]' and needs a specific named person as the standard — here we are ranking mom against the entire family.
だけが would mean 'only mom wakes up early in the family,' implying nobody else does — but the sentence is saying she wakes up earliest among many, not that she's the only early riser.
exercises.hiragana_full: かぞくのなかでははがいちばんはやくおきます。

audio_alternatives.kanji: 母はうちの家族で一番早く起きる人だ。
audio_alternatives.hiragana_full: はははうちのかぞくでいちばんはやくおきるひとだ。

audio_alternatives.kanji: 毎朝、家族の誰よりも母が早く起きる。
audio_alternatives.hiragana_full: まいあさ、かぞくのだれよりもははがはやくおきる。

exercises.example_sentence: 日本の都市の中で東京が一番大きいです。
exercises.translation: Out of Japan's cities, Tokyo is the biggest.
wrong_choice_explanations: のほうが sets up a head-to-head comparison ('Tokyo is bigger'), but the sentence is ranking Tokyo at the top of every Japanese city, which calls for 一番.
よりも means 'bigger than [a specific city]' and needs a second city named as the reference; here Tokyo is being compared against the whole group.
だけが would mean 'only Tokyo is big among Japan's cities,' but the sentence is saying Tokyo is the biggest — other cities like Osaka are also big, just not as big.
exercises.hiragana_full: にほんのとしのなかでとうきょうがいちばんおおきいです。

audio_alternatives.kanji: 日本で一番大きい都市は東京です。
audio_alternatives.hiragana_full: にほんでいちばんおおきいとしはとうきょうです。

audio_alternatives.kanji: 東京は日本のどの都市よりも大きい。
audio_alternatives.hiragana_full: とうきょうはにほんのどのとしよりもおおきい。

exercises.example_sentence: スポーツの中でサッカーが一番人気があります。
exercises.translation: Out of all sports, soccer is the most popular.
wrong_choice_explanations: のほうが sets up a two-way comparison ('soccer is more popular'), but the sentence is naming soccer as the most popular among all sports, which requires 一番.
よりも means 'more than [a specific sport]' and needs a second sport as the standard; the sentence is comparing soccer to the whole sports category.
だけが would mean 'only soccer is popular among sports,' but the sentence is just saying it is the most popular — basketball and baseball can still be popular, just less so.
exercises.hiragana_full: すぽーつのなかでさっかーがいちばんにんきがあります。

audio_alternatives.kanji: サッカーはあらゆるスポーツの中で最も人気がある。
audio_alternatives.hiragana_full: さっかーはあらゆるすぽーつのなかでもっともにんきがある。

audio_alternatives.kanji: 数あるスポーツでサッカーほど人気のあるものはない。
audio_alternatives.hiragana_full: かずあるすぽーつでさっかーほどにんきのあるものはない。

exercises.example_sentence: 友達の中で彼が一番おもしろいです。
exercises.translation: Out of my friends, he is the funniest.
wrong_choice_explanations: のほうが sets up a two-way comparison ('he is funnier'), but the sentence is ranking him at the top of the speaker's whole friend group, which calls for 一番.
よりも means 'funnier than [someone specific]' and needs another named person; here he is being compared against the entire group of friends.
だけが would mean 'only he is funny among the friends,' implying the others aren't funny at all — but the sentence is saying he is the funniest, not that he is alone in being funny.
exercises.hiragana_full: ともだちのなかでかれがいちばんおもしろいです。

audio_alternatives.kanji: わたしの友達で一番おもしろいのは彼だ。
audio_alternatives.hiragana_full: わたしのともだちでいちばんおもしろいのはかれだ。

audio_alternatives.kanji: 彼はわたしの友達の誰よりもおもしろい人です。
audio_alternatives.hiragana_full: かれはわたしのともだちのだれよりもおもしろいひとです。

exercises.example_sentence: 季節の中で春が一番好きです。
exercises.translation: Out of the seasons, I like spring the best.
wrong_choice_explanations: のほうが sets up a two-way comparison ('I like spring more'), but the sentence is picking spring as the top favorite out of all four seasons, which requires 一番.
よりも means 'more than [another season]' and needs a second season as the reference; the sentence is comparing spring against the whole seasonal lineup.
だけが would mean 'I only like spring among the seasons,' implying the speaker dislikes the others — but they are simply saying spring is the favorite, not the only one they like.
exercises.hiragana_full: きせつのなかではるがいちばんすきです。

audio_alternatives.kanji: わたしは四季の中で春が一番好きだ。
audio_alternatives.hiragana_full: わたしはしきのなかではるがいちばんすきだ。

audio_alternatives.kanji: 春はわたしにとってどの季節よりも好きな時期です。
audio_alternatives.hiragana_full: はるはわたしにとってどのきせつよりもすきなじきです。

exercises.example_sentence: この店のケーキの中でチョコレートケーキが一番おいしいです。
exercises.translation: Out of this shop's cakes, the chocolate cake is the tastiest.
wrong_choice_explanations: のほうが sets up a two-way comparison ('the chocolate cake is tastier'), but the sentence is ranking it as the best among all the cakes in this shop, which requires 一番.
よりも means 'tastier than [a specific cake]' and needs another named cake; here it is being compared to the whole lineup at the shop.
だけが would mean 'only the chocolate cake is tasty here,' but the sentence is just naming it the tastiest — the other cakes may also be tasty, just not as tasty.
exercises.hiragana_full: このみせのけーきのなかでちょこれーとけーきがいちばんおいしいです。

audio_alternatives.kanji: このお店で一番おいしいケーキはチョコレートケーキです。
audio_alternatives.hiragana_full: このおみせでいちばんおいしいけーきはちょこれーとけーきです。

audio_alternatives.kanji: ここのケーキでチョコレートケーキほどおいしいものはない。
audio_alternatives.hiragana_full: ここのけーきでちょこれーとけーきほどおいしいものはない。

exercises.example_sentence: 兄弟の中で弟が一番元気だ。
exercises.translation: Out of the siblings, my little brother is the most energetic.
wrong_choice_explanations: のほうが sets up a two-way comparison ('my brother is more energetic'), but the sentence is ranking him at the top of all the siblings, which calls for 一番.
よりも means 'more energetic than [a specific sibling]' and needs another named person; here he is being compared against the whole sibling group.
だけが would mean 'only my little brother has energy among the siblings,' but the sentence is saying he is the most energetic, not that the others have no energy.
exercises.hiragana_full: きょうだいのなかでおとうとがいちばんげんきだ。

audio_alternatives.kanji: うちの兄弟で一番元気なのは弟だ。
audio_alternatives.hiragana_full: うちのきょうだいでいちばんげんきなのはおとうとだ。

audio_alternatives.kanji: 弟は兄弟の中で誰よりも元気いっぱいだ。
audio_alternatives.hiragana_full: おとうとはきょうだいのなかでだれよりもげんきいっぱいだ。

exercises.example_sentence: 世界の動物の中でチーターが一番速いです。
exercises.translation: Out of all the world's animals, the cheetah is the fastest.
wrong_choice_explanations: のほうが sets up a two-way comparison ('the cheetah is faster'), but the sentence is naming it the fastest of every animal in the world, which requires 一番.
よりも means 'faster than [a specific animal]' and needs another named animal as the standard; here the cheetah is being compared against the whole animal kingdom.
だけが would mean 'only the cheetah is fast in the world,' but the sentence is just claiming the top speed — lions and horses can still be fast, just not as fast as the cheetah.
exercises.hiragana_full: せかいのどうぶつのなかでちーたーがいちばんはやいです。

audio_alternatives.kanji: 陸上の動物で一番速く走れるのはチーターだ。
audio_alternatives.hiragana_full: りくじょうのどうぶつでいちばんはやくはしれるのはちーたーだ。

audio_alternatives.kanji: チーターはどんな動物よりも速く走ることができる。
audio_alternatives.hiragana_full: ちーたーはどんなどうぶつよりもはやくはしることができる。
